regions: Pacific slope of western South America
nativeRange.habitat: Coastal streams and rivers of the Pacific slope, generally over sandy substrate with rock and woody cover.
nativeRange.biotope: South American coastal river / stream biotope with sandy substrate, driftwood, and scattered rockwork.

decorPreferences: avoid delicate rooted plants (they tend to dig)

diet.notes: Generally accepts a varied diet of sinking cichlid pellets, frozen and live foods (earthworms, prawns, mussel, krill), and occasional vegetable matter such as blanched peas or spinach. They have been reported to do best on a moderate-protein diet, and very fatty mammalian meats are commonly advised against.

goodWith: other large, robust South/Central American cichlids in suitably sized tanks
avoidWith: Corydoras and other small bottom dwellers
African rift-lake cichlids (very different water and behaviour)

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Hans Dam, 2048x1365. Tack-sharp full lateral of a vividly coloured adult: electric-blue metallic spangling, blue facial vermiculation, dark midbody blotch, and bright orange (gold-saum) margins on dorsal, caudal and anal fins. Filename uses the accepted synonym Aequidens rivulatus. The most striking, saturated specimen in the pool.

Photo by 5snake5, 4363x2544. Sharp full-body lateral of a male showing the diagnostic gold/orange margin along the dorsal and caudal fins, dense turquoise spangling and blue facial squiggles. Different individual/angle for variety.

Photo by H. Zell, 5391x3028 (highest resolution). Crisp full lateral of a gold-green individual with a prominent orange caudal-fin margin, black lateral spot and blue head markings. Third distinct individual.

summary: The Green Terror is a large, vividly marked South American cichlid native to Pacific-slope rivers of Ecuador and Peru. Generally considered an intermediate-level species, it is highly territorial and best kept singly or as a bonded pair in a spacious aquarium.
careNotes:
Two distinct forms of the Green Terror are traded under that name, and it is worth knowing the difference. The fish most commonly sold is Andinoacara rivulatus, known in the hobby as the 'Gold Saum' Green Terror, recognised by an orange-to-gold margin along the dorsal and caudal fins. The very similar 'Silver Saum' Green Terror is now recognised as a separate species, Andinoacara stalsbergi, distinguished by a white fin margin and a more restricted range in Peru. Most fish sold simply as 'Green Terror' are A. rivulatus; anyone specifically seeking the Silver Saum should confirm the species identity with the seller before purchasing.

Adult size is reported across a range of 20–30 cm, with some sources noting smaller typical aquarium sizes and others recording larger specimens measured in the wild. A single Green Terror is generally considered manageable in a tank of around 300 L with a footprint of at least 150 cm in length; a bonded pair or a community of large cichlids realistically requires 450–650 L or more, and specialist recommendations can run higher still once tankmates are present.

Conspecific aggression is consistently described as very high. Untested males have commonly been reported to kill females if introduced directly, so a widely recommended approach is to raise a group of six or more juveniles together and allow a compatible pair to form naturally, then rehome the remaining fish once the pair has established. The Green Terror is not suited to tanks containing small or delicate species.
breedingNotes: Has been reported to breed readily in captivity once a compatible pair has formed. Both parents tend the eggs and fry, with the female fanning and the male defending the territory; eggs generally hatch in 3–4 days and fry are free-swimming around 6–8 days. Breeding pairs become extremely aggressive toward other tank inhabitants and may kill tankmates, so a dedicated breeding tank is commonly advised.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Swapped to primary because the configured slice primary (Aquarium Co-Op) has no Green Terror care page — its on-site search returned only merchandise/unrelated articles, and direct guesses at /blogs/aquarium/green-terror-cichlid and /products/green-terror both returned 404.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for authoritative taxonomy (Cichlidae / Cichliformes), distribution, IUCN status (Least Concern, 2020), max length (20 cm TL), and breeding biology (up to ~600 eggs, biparental care).

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Hobby cross-check on adult size (~20 cm aquarium / ~30 cm wild), lifespan (7–10 years), care level (intermediate), and the Gold Saum vs Silver Saum (A. stalsbergi) trade distinction. Quoted minimum tank size of 35 US gal single / 75 US gal pair is markedly smaller than SeriouslyFish's ~650 L figure.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping
additional.notes: Attempted as a fourth cross-check; site returned HTTP 403 Forbidden and could not be consulted.

fish.breedingNotes: Pairs spawn on flat surfaces such as flat rocks or the aquarium floor, with both parents providing biparental care of the eggs and fry. Compatible pair formation is the main challenge — the most commonly recommended approach is to raise six or more juveniles together and remove the remaining fish once a pair has naturally established.
